title: Plotting maps with iNaturalist and temperature data
text:
Code
# packages
library(tidyverse)
library(rinat)
library(geodata)
library(elevatr)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tidyterra)

title: Plotting maps with iNaturalist and temperature data
text:
Context
I often find myself needing to quickly download and plot iNaturalist data for a given species, and I also often want to plot temperature maps for a given region. Below is a quick example of how to do so for western fence lizards in the area in and around Reno, Nevada.

title: Plotting maps with iNaturalist and temperature data
text:
Downloading iNaturalist data
I will use the get_inat_obs() function from the rinat package to download iNaturalist observations of western fence lizards in the Reno area. Note that get_inat_obs() has a lot of options for filtering the data and can be pretty slow if you are downloading a lot of data.


Code
# define bound box for Reno area
reno_bbox <- c(39.25, -120.5, 39.75, -119.5)

# download iNaturalist observations of western fence lizards in the Reno area
lizard_obs <- get_inat_obs(
  taxon_name = "Sceloporus occidentalis",
  quality = "research", # iNat research quality only
  bounds = reno_bbox, # must be S,W,N,E order
  maxresults = 2000 # max can only be 10K
)


To have a pretty background, I will also download elevation data from the same area using the get_elev_raster() function from the geodata package.


Code
# define grid for elevation data
elev_grid <- expand.grid(
  lat = seq(reno_bbox[1], reno_bbox[3], by = 0.01),
  lon = seq(reno_bbox[2], reno_bbox[4], by = 0.01)
)

# set elevations ar sf object
elev_grid_sf <- st_as_sf(elev_grid, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

# download elevation data as a raster
elev_raster <- elevatr::get_elev_raster(elev_grid_sf, z = 8, clip = "bbox")


Mosaicing & Projecting


Clipping DEM to bbox


Note: Elevation units are in meters.


Code
# transform elevation raster to data frame
elev_df <- as.data.frame(elev_raster, xy = TRUE) |> rename(elevation = 3)

# remove negative elevations (i.e., water)
elev_df <- elev_df |> filter(elevation >= 0)


Now let’s plot the data. Note that I am using the scale_fill_whitebox_c() function from terra to obtain a nice color palette for elevation data. Check more elevation color palettes here.


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = elev_df, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = elevation)) +
  geom_contour(
    data = elev_df, aes(x = x, y = y, z = elevation),
    color = "white", alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(palette = "high_relief") +
  geom_point(
    data = lizard_obs, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude), color = "darkred"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = c(0, 0), 
    breaks = seq(-120.5, -119.5, by = 0.25),
    labels = paste(seq(-120.5, -119.5, by = 0.25), "°W")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = c(0, 0),
    breaks = seq(39.25, 39.75, by = 0.25),
    labels = paste(seq(39.25, 39.75, by = 0.25), "°N")
  ) +
  labs(fill = "Elevation (m)") +
  coord_map(
    xlim = c(reno_bbox[2], reno_bbox[4]),
    ylim = c(reno_bbox[1], reno_bbox[3])
  ) +
  theme(axis.title = element_blank())

title: Plotting maps with iNaturalist and temperature data
text:
Downloading and plotting temperature data
Next, I will use the geodata package to download temperature data for the same area. I will use the worldclim_country() function to download climate data at a resolution of 0.5 minutes for the US, and then I will crop the data to the Reno area using the terra package.

Note: Downloaidng global climate data can take a while and will be a big file. Set the path = tempdir() argument to download the data to a temporary directory that will be deleted after the R session ends. If you want to keep the data, set path to a directory on your computer where you want to save the data.



Code
# Note: Not running this here because it takes a while to download data.

# download global climate data at 1 minute resolution
climate_data <- geodata::worldclim_country(
  var = "tavg", # average temperature
  res = 0.5, # resolution in minutes (0.5 = 30 seconds)
  country = "usa", # country to download data for
  path = tempdir(), # download to temporary directory
)

# crop climate data to Reno area
climate_data_cropped <- terra::crop(
  climate_data,
  terra::ext(reno_bbox[2], reno_bbox[4], reno_bbox[1], reno_bbox[3]) # must be W,E,S,N order # nolint
)

# get only data for June (i.e., month 6)
climate_data_cropped <- climate_data_cropped[[6]]

# transform climate data to data frame
climate_df <- as.data.frame(climate_data_cropped, xy = TRUE) |>
  rename(temperature = 3)

# save climate_df to .rda file
save(climate_df, file = "posts/inat-and-temp-data/climate_df.rda")


Now let’s plot the temperature data.


Code
# load saved climate data
load("climate_df.rda")

# plot temperature data
climate_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, fill = temperature)) +
  geom_raster() +
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(palette = "muted") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = c(0, 0),
    breaks = seq(-120.5, -119.5, by = 0.25),
    labels = paste0(seq(-120.5, -119.5, by = 0.25), "°W")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = c(0, 0),
    breaks = seq(39.25, 39.75, by = 0.25),
    labels = paste(seq(39.25, 39.75, by = 0.25), "°N")
  ) +
  labs(fill = "Avg. June\nTemperature (°C)") +
  coord_map(
    xlim = c(reno_bbox[2], reno_bbox[4]),
    ylim = c(reno_bbox[1], reno_bbox[3])
  ) +
  theme(axis.title = element_blank())

title: Expansion of Podarcis muralis in British Columbia
Expansion of Podarcis muralis in British Columbia
text:
Context
The Common Wall Lizard (Podarcis muralis), a small lizard native to Europe, has become a conspicuous new resident in many parts of British Columbia. Since its introduction to Vancouver Island in the 1970s from a small founding population, the lizard rapidly expanded. Its range has grown exponentially, now covering a large portion of the island and with satellite populations appearing on the mainland and surrounding islands.
This exploration stems from my interest in putting together a CIRTA grant proposal together with some folks at UBC to study the dynamics of this invasion. The abstract of the proposal was as follows:

Canada’s harsh winters have historically acted as a natural barrier to many invasive animal species that flourish elsewhere. However, climate change is shifting these thermal boundaries, putting ecosystems that were once safe at risk. Predicting poleward range expansion is challenging because standard correlative methods often omit factors such as physiology, behavior, and the ability for local adaptation, which can help invasive animals succeed in seemingly unsuitable climates. Therefore, there is an urgent need for mechanistic frameworks that go beyond correlation to improve predictions of invasive spread. I propose to develop such a framework using the invasion of the common wall lizard (Podarcis muralis) on Vancouver Island as a model system. Despite apparent mismatches in climate for this Mediterranean ectotherm, P. muralis has rapidly expanded across British Columbia since its introduction in 1970. Now present on the Canadian mainland, it poses a serious ecological threat by outcompeting native reptiles and disrupting local food webs through insect predation. Based at the University of British Columbia, I will first assess the current range and traits of the invading lizards. In partnership with Dr. Katie Marshall and Dr. Chris Harley, I will then use ecophysiological modeling to understand how local climates and habitats influence P. muralis’s physiology and behavior. These insights will be incorporated into a hierarchical Bayesian framework, developed with Dr. Marie Auger-Methé, to discern the mechanisms underlying the spread of P. muralis while rigorously accounting for observational biases and spatiotemporal autocorrelations. Beyond generating habitat suitability maps to forecast the wall lizard’s future spread, this research will establish a transferable, scalable framework for modeling the colonization of high-latitude regions by non-native ectotherms. Ultimately, the proposed framework will provide a mechanistic basis for assessing invasion risks across Canada’s shifting latitudinal gradients, including the increasingly vulnerable Arctic and sub-Arctic regions.

title: Expansion of Podarcis muralis in British Columbia
text:
Data exploration
Below, I explore some data I obtained from iNaturalist to visualize the expansion of Podarcis muralis in BC. The data includes observations of the species, with associated metadata such as location and date of observation.


Read and clean data
# read data
podarcis <- read.csv("podarcis-obs-bc.csv")

# remove observations with missing coordinates
podarcis <- podarcis |>
  filter(!is.na(longitude) & !is.na(latitude)) |>
  mutate(year = year(ymd_hms(time_observed_at))) |>
  mutate(month = month(ymd_hms(time_observed_at))) |>
  filter(!is.na(year)) |>
  filter(longitude < -122)

# sort by year
podarcis <- podarcis[order(-podarcis$year), ]


Next, I use the function ne_countries() from the rnaturalearth package to get the country borders for Canada and the USA, which I will use as a base layer for plotting the observations.


Get country borders for plotting
# get canada border
canada_border <- ne_countries(
  country = "canada",
  scale = "large",
  returnclass = "sf"
)

# get usa border
usa_border <- ne_countries(
  country = "united states of america",
  scale = "large",
  returnclass = "sf"
)


With all that, I plot the observations of Podarcis muralis over space, using the country borders as a base layer.


Plot observations over space
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = canada_border, fill = "lightgray", color = "black") +
  geom_sf(data = usa_border, fill = "lightgray", color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = podarcis, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, color = year)) +
  scale_color_viridis(option = "viridis", discrete = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue"),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-126, -122), ylim = c(48, 50.5)) +
  labs(color = "Year") +
  theme(axis.title = element_blank())









It looks like the origin of the invasion is located in southern Vancouver Island. Expansion proceeds northwards and eastwards towards the mainland. It seems to have gone all around the straight of Georgia and not by island-hopping through the Gulf Islands. As of recent years, it seems to have gone all the way to the mainland and is now present in the city of Vancouver.
Below I plot the number of observations per year to visualize the temporal expansion of the species.


Plot observations over time
podarcis |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(count = n()) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = count)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of Observations")









As with most iNaturalist data, there is a strong bias towards recent year.
Lastly, I plot the observations by month, standardized by year to visualize the seasonal pattern of observations.


Plot observations by month
podarcis |>
  group_by(year, month) |>
  summarise(count = n()) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(relative_count = count / sum(count)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = year, fill = relative_count)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = count), size = 3, color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis() +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12, labels = month.abb, expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = seq(2005, 2025, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Month", y = "Year", fill = "Proportion")









Looks like it peaks in April and again in August-September, although with some year-to-year variation specially between 2019 and 2025 when there is full data coverage.

title: Welcome to backburner!
text:
In this blog, I will be creating new posts with the intention of exploring random topics that I find interesting. Some of these topics will be research-related, while others may be just random passing thoughts I decided to waste my time on. The goal is to have fun, learn new things and store all of these random explorations in one place.






   
    
    
      Order By
      Default
      
        Date - Oldest
      
      
        Date - Newest
      
      
        Title
